question_text: In the Antiquarian Library of St. Cuthbert's College, two magnificent leather-bound volumes of the Complete Oxford Dictionary sit side by side on a mahogany shelf, arranged in the standard way — Volume I on the left and Volume II on the right, with their gilded spines facing outward toward the reader. Each volume consists of exactly 1,000 pages of fine India paper, with the pages measuring precisely 2 inches thick when compressed between the covers. Each cover — front and back of each volume — is exactly one-quarter inch thick. A rare and troublesome bookworm named Lexington begins eating at page 1 of Volume I and chews in a perfectly straight horizontal line through to the very last page of Volume II. Assuming Lexington eats through covers and pages alike, how many inches of material does Lexington eat through in total?
